_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Signs of dehydration (as per 'Some' or 'Severe' dehydration criteria, e.g. restless/irritable, sunken eyes, drinks eagerly/thirsty, skin pinch - goes back very slowly for 'Some Dehydration'; or lethargy/unconscious, sunken eyes, not able to drink/drinking poorly, skin pinch - goes back slowly for 'Severe Dehydration')

preauth.label: Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment

ppd_preauth.q: Were the Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment submitted?
Was the Sr. Electrolyte report submitted?
Did the patient complain of > 3 watery stool per day?
Did the Sr. Electrolyte report suggest hyponatremic dehydration (Na <135 mmol/L)/ Hypernatremic dehydration (Na> 145 mmol/L)?

cpd_claim.q: Were the Detailed Indoor case papers submitted with daily vitals and line of treatment?
Was post treatment stool culture report submitted?
Were post treatment Sr. Electrolyte submitted?
Was the detailed Discharge Summary submitted with date of the follow-up mentioned?

common_queries: Patient did not complain of > 3 watery stool per day.
Sr. Electrolyte report not suggestive of hyponatremic (Na <135 mmol/L) or hypernatremic (Na >145 mmol/L) dehydration.
Clinical notes missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
CBC report not submitted at pre-authorization.
Sr. Electrolyte report not submitted at pre-authorization.
Post treatment Stool culture report submitted at claim stage (when not required).
Post treatment Sr. Electrolyte report not submitted at claim stage.

referral_criteria: Dysentery with age <1 yr/ measles in past 6 weeks/dehydration/sick
Dysentery with no improvement on antibiotics
Persistent diarrhea with serious systemic infection such as pneumonia, sepsis, infants <4 months of age, or when there is no improvement with treatment over 5 days
If hydration status is not improving, give IV drip more rapidly
In hypernatremic and hyponatremic dehydration child appears relatively less ill / more ill respectively and needs to be referred for hospitalization
Persistent Diarrhoea: Refer to hospital (See box)
contraindications: Unsuitable fluids: carbonated beverages, commercial fruit juice, sweetened tea & coffee, other medicinal teas/infusions.
For Persistent Diarrhoea, avoid very sweet foods or drinks.
